title: Check Eulerian graph
description: Check if graph has Euler path or circuit

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0:[1,4], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3,0]. Step 2: Find start vertex with degree>0: vertex 0. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, visit neighbors 1 and 4; stack=[1,4], pop 4, visit neighbor 3; stack=[1,3], pop 3, visit neighbor 2; stack=[1,2], pop 2, all neighbors visited; stack=[1], pop 1, all neighbors visited. Visited: [True,True,True,True,True]. Step 4: Check connectivity: all vertices with degree>0 (all 5) visited. Step 5: Count odd degrees: deg(0)=2 even, deg(1)=2 even, deg(2)=2 even, deg(3)=2 even, deg(4)=2 even, odd_count=0. Step 6: odd_count=0 → Euler circuit.

Step 1: Adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]. Step 2: Start vertex: 0. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, visit neighbor 1; stack=[1], pop 1, visit neighbor 2; stack=[2], pop 2, visit neighbor 3; stack=[3], pop 3, no unvisited neighbors. Visited: [True,True,True,True]. Step 4: All vertices with degree>0 visited. Step 5: Count odd degrees: deg(0)=1 odd, deg(1)=2 even, deg(2)=2 even, deg(3)=1 odd, odd_count=2. Step 6: odd_count=2 → Euler path.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V+E) with DFS
approaches.summary: Perform DFS to check connectivity of the graph, then count degrees of all vertices to apply Euler's theorems. It directly implements the mathematical conditions without optimizations for degree counting.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Eulerian Graph with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if a graph has an Euler circuit or path using DFS connectivity check and degree counting.
ladders.explanation: This solution directly implements Euler's theorems through a two-phase approach. First, we need to verify the graph's connectivity for vertices with non-zero degree using DFS—this ensures all relevant vertices are reachable. Second, we count the degrees of all vertices to apply Euler's conditions: for an Euler circuit, all vertices must have even degree; for an Euler path, exactly zero or two vertices must have odd degree. The intuition is that Euler's theorems provide necessary and sufficient conditions based purely on degree parity and connectivity, making brute force counting sufficient. The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from input edges. 2) Find a starting vertex with non-zero degree. 3) Perform DFS from that vertex to mark all reachable vertices with non-zero degree. 4) Check if all vertices with non-zero degree were visited—if not, return 'none'. 5) Count odd-degree vertices. 6) If odd count is 0, return 'circuit'; if odd count is 2, return 'path'; else return 'none'. Time complexity is O(V+E) for DFS and degree counting. Space is O(V+E) for adjacency list and visited array. This connects to L1 by using the DFS connectivity check as a subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle disconnected vertices with zero degree (they don't affect connectivity) or misinterpreting the odd-degree count condition. Real code pattern: def is_eulerian(vertices, edges): adj = [[] for _ in range(vertices)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u); start = -1; for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) > 0: start = i; break; if start == -1: return 'circuit' (trivial empty graph); visited = [False]*vertices; stack = [start]; visited[start] = True; while stack: node = stack.pop(); for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor] = True; stack.append(neighbor); for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) > 0 and not visited[i]: return 'none'; odd_count = 0; for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) % 2 == 1: odd_count += 1; if odd_count == 0: return 'circuit'; elif odd_count == 2: return 'path'; else: return 'none'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0:[1,4], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3,0]. Step 2: Find start vertex with degree>0: vertex 0. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, visit neighbors 1 and 4; stack=[1,4], pop 4, visit neighbor 3; stack=[1,3], pop 3, visit neighbor 2; stack=[1,2], pop 2, all neighbors visited; stack=[1], pop 1, all neighbors visited. Visited: [True,True,True,True,True]. Step 4: Check connectivity: all vertices with degree>0 (all 5) visited. Step 5: Count odd degrees: deg(0)=2 even, deg(1)=2 even, deg(2)=2 even, deg(3)=2 even, deg(4)=2 even, odd_count=0. Step 6: odd_count=0 → Euler circuit.

Step 1: Adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]. Step 2: Start vertex: 0. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, visit neighbor 1; stack=[1], pop 1, visit neighbor 2; stack=[2], pop 2, visit neighbor 3; stack=[3], pop 3, no unvisited neighbors. Visited: [True,True,True,True]. Step 4: All vertices with degree>0 visited. Step 5: Count odd degrees: deg(0)=1 odd, deg(1)=2 even, deg(2)=2 even, deg(3)=1 odd, odd_count=2. Step 6: odd_count=2 → Euler path.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Connectivity Check for Non-Zero Degree Vertices
ladders.desc: Perform DFS to verify all vertices with edges are connected in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine ensures the graph is 'connected' for the purpose of Eulerian conditions, meaning all vertices with at least one edge must be reachable from each other. The intuition is that Euler paths/circuits require traversing all edges exactly once, so if there are disconnected components with edges, it's impossible. We use DFS because it naturally explores reachability. The algorithm: 1) Given adjacency list and number of vertices. 2) Find any vertex with degree > 0 as start (if none, graph has no edges and is trivially connected). 3) Initialize visited array. 4) Perform DFS from start using stack or recursion to mark all reachable vertices. 5) After DFS, check if every vertex with degree > 0 was visited; if any such vertex is unvisited, the graph is disconnected for Eulerian purposes. Time complexity is O(V+E) for DFS traversal. Space is O(V) for visited array plus O(V+E) for adjacency list. This feeds into L0 by providing the connectivity verification step. Common mistakes include starting DFS from vertex 0 regardless of degree (might be isolated) or including zero-degree vertices in the connectivity check (they don't matter). Real code pattern: def is_connected(adj, vertices): start = -1; for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) > 0: start = i; break; if start == -1: return True; visited = [False]*vertices; stack = [start]; visited[start] = True; while stack: node = stack.pop(); for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor] = True; stack.append(neighbor); for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) > 0 and not visited[i]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find start vertex with degree>0: vertex 0 (degree 1). Step 2: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False]. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, mark visited[0]=True, push neighbor 1; stack=[1], pop 1, mark visited[1]=True, push neighbors 0 (visited) and 2; stack=[2], pop 2, mark visited[2]=True, push neighbors 1 (visited) and 3; stack=[3], pop 3, mark visited[3]=True, push neighbor 2 (visited). Step 4: Check vertices with degree>0: all vertices have degree>0, all visited → connected.

Step 1: Find start: vertex 0 (degree 1). Step 2: visited=[False,False,False,False,False]. Step 3: DFS from 0: stack=[0], pop 0, visited[0]=True, push neighbor 1; stack=[1], pop 1, visited[1]=True, push neighbor 0 (visited). Step 4: Check vertices with degree>0: vertices 0,1,2,3 have degree>0 (vertex 4 has degree 0). Visited: [True,True,False,False,False]. Vertices 2 and 3 not visited → disconnected.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Odd-Degree Vertices for Euler's Theorems
ladders.desc: Count vertices with odd degree to determine Euler path or circuit conditions.
ladders.explanation: This is the central mathematical insight: Euler's theorems state that for an undirected graph, an Euler circuit exists if and only if all vertices have even degree and the graph is connected (for non-zero degree vertices). An Euler path exists if and only if exactly zero or two vertices have odd degree and the graph is connected. The intuition is that odd-degree vertices must be endpoints of a path (if two exist) or none for a circuit, due to parity constraints in edge traversal. The algorithm is straightforward: iterate through all vertices, compute degree from adjacency list, and count how many have odd degree (degree % 2 == 1). Time complexity is O(V) since we check each vertex once. Space is O(1) for the counter. This feeds into L1 and L0 by providing the degree analysis after connectivity is verified. Common mistakes include miscounting degrees in undirected graphs (each edge contributes 1 to degree of both endpoints) or forgetting that zero odd-degree vertices can indicate either circuit or path (circuit takes precedence). Real code pattern: def count_odd_degrees(adj, vertices): odd_count = 0; for i in range(vertices): if len(adj[i]) % 2 == 1: odd_count += 1; return odd_count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute degrees: vertex 0 degree=3? Wait, adj[0]=[1,2,3] so degree=3 (odd). But let's verify: actually, adj as given: 0 connects to 1,2,3 → degree 3 (odd). 1 connects to 0,2 → degree 2 (even). 2 connects to 0,1,3 → degree 3 (odd). 3 connects to 0,2 → degree 2 (even). Odd count: vertices 0 and 2 → 2. Correction: output should be 2. Let's re-trace correctly: adj[0]=[1,2,3] degree 3 odd, adj[1]=[0,2] degree 2 even, adj[2]=[0,1,3] degree 3 odd, adj[3]=[0,2] degree 2 even. Odd count = 2.
Step 1: Degrees: vertex 0: [1,4] degree 2 even; vertex 1: [0,2] degree 2 even; vertex 2: [1,3] degree 2 even; vertex 3: [2,4] degree 2 even; vertex 4: [3,0] degree 2 even. All even → odd_count=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Constructing Adjacency List from Edge List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency list representation for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate step is crucial because most graph algorithms, including DFS, operate efficiently on adjacency lists. An adjacency list stores, for each vertex, a list of its neighboring vertices. For an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) adds v to u's list and u to v's list. The intuition is that this representation allows O(degree) access to neighbors, which is optimal for traversal algorithms. The algorithm: initialize an empty list of lists of size V. For each edge (u,v) in the input edge list, append v to adj[u] and append u to adj[v]. Time complexity is O(E) since we process each edge once. Space is O(V+E) for the adjacency list. This feeds into L2 and L1 by providing the graph representation needed for degree counting and DFS. Common mistakes include forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs or using incorrect data structures (e.g., matrix for sparse graphs). Real code pattern: def build_adjacency_list(vertices, edges): adj = [[] for _ in range(vertices)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u); return adj.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj = [[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0] → adj[0]=[1]; append 0 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: append 2 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0,2]; append 1 to adj[2] → adj[2]=[1]. Step 4: Process edge [2,3]: append 3 to adj[2] → adj[2]=[1,3]; append 2 to adj[3] → adj[3]=[2]. Final adj: [[1],[0,2],[1,3],[2]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Graph Vertices and Edges
ladders.desc: Fundamental loops to access all vertices and their incident edges in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing the graph structure, which is essential for any graph algorithm. For adjacency list representation, we typically use two nested loops: an outer loop over all vertices (0 to V-1), and an inner loop over the adjacency list of each vertex to access its edges. The intuition is that this enumerates all edges exactly twice (once per endpoint in undirected graphs) and allows operations like degree computation or neighbor processing. The algorithm: for i in range(vertices): for neighbor in adj[i]: process edge (i, neighbor). Time complexity is O(V+E) since each vertex and edge is visited. Space is O(1) for the iteration itself. This feeds into L3 by being the mechanism to build or use adjacency lists. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in vertex indices or modifying the adjacency list while iterating. Real code pattern: def iterate_graph(adj, vertices): for i in range(vertices): print(f'Vertex {i}:'); for neighbor in adj[i]: print(f'  edge to {neighbor}').

examples.output: Vertex 0: edge to 1, edge to 2; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 0, edge to 1
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, adj[0]=[1,2]: print edge to 1, edge to 2. Step 2: i=1, adj[1]=[0,2]: print edge to 0, edge to 2. Step 3: i=2, adj[2]=[0,1]: print edge to 0, edge to 1.

examples.output: Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 1, edge to 3; Vertex 3: edge to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, adj[0]=[1]: print edge to 1. Step 2: i=1, adj[1]=[0,2]: print edge to 0, edge to 2. Step 3: i=2, adj[2]=[1,3]: print edge to 1, edge to 3. Step 4: i=3, adj[3]=[2]: print edge to 2.

testCases.expected: Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0
Vertex 0: edge to 1, edge to 2, edge to 3; Vertex 1: edge to 0; Vertex 2: edge to 0; Vertex 3: edge to 0
Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 1, edge to 3; Vertex 3: edge to 2, edge to 4; Vertex 4: edge to 3
Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 1
Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 1, edge to 3; Vertex 3: edge to 2, edge to 4; Vertex 4: edge to 3, edge to 5; Vertex 5: edge to 4
Vertex 0: edge to 1, edge to 2; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 3; Vertex 2: edge to 0; Vertex 3: edge to 1
Vertex 0: edge to 1, edge to 4; Vertex 1: edge to 0, edge to 2; Vertex 2: edge to 1, edge to 3; Vertex 3: edge to 2, edge to 4; Vertex 4: edge to 3, edge to 0
Vertex 0: edge to 1; Vertex 1: edge to 0; Vertex 2: edge to 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Degree in Undirected Graphs
ladders.desc: Understand degree of a vertex: number of edges incident to it.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: in an undirected graph, the degree of a vertex is the count of edges connected to it. Each edge contributes 1 to the degree of both its endpoints. For example, if vertex A is connected to vertices B and C, its degree is 2. The intuition is that degree measures how many connections a vertex has, which is fundamental to Eulerian problems because parity (even/odd) of degrees determines traversal possibilities. To compute degree from an adjacency list, simply take the length of the list for that vertex. From an edge list, count how many edges include the vertex. Time complexity for degree lookup is O(1) with adjacency list. Space is part of graph representation. This feeds into L4 as the basic property accessed during iteration. Common mistakes include confusing degree with other metrics like indegree/outdegree in directed graphs or miscounting self-loops (not considered here). Real code pattern: def degree(adj, vertex): return len(adj[vertex]).

examples.trace: Step 1: For vertex 0, adj[0] = [1,2,3]. Step 2: Count elements: 1,2,3 → 3 elements. Step 3: Degree = 3.
Step 1: For vertex 1, adj[1] = [0,2,3]. Step 2: Count: 0,2,3 → 3 elements. Step 3: Degree = 3.

approaches.name: Degree Check with Union-Find O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use Union-Find to efficiently check graph connectivity in near-constant time, then count vertex degrees to determine Euler path or circuit. This optimizes connectivity verification compared to DFS.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Eulerian Graph Using Degree Check with Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if a graph has an Euler circuit, Euler path, or neither using degree counting and Union-Find connectivity check.
ladders.explanation: This solution determines Eulerian properties by combining two key checks: connectivity and vertex degree analysis. First, we verify the graph is connected (or has at most one non-trivial connected component) using Union-Find, which efficiently handles connectivity queries in near-constant time. Then, we count the degrees of all vertices. For an undirected graph: an Euler circuit exists if all vertices have even degrees and the graph is connected; an Euler path exists if exactly zero or two vertices have odd degrees and the graph is connected (or has at most one non-trivial component); otherwise, neither exists. The intuition is that Euler circuits require entering and exiting each vertex equally (even degrees), while Euler paths allow exactly two endpoints with odd degrees (start and end vertices). Union-Find optimizes connectivity checking compared to DFS by avoiding full graph traversal for each query, crucial for sparse graphs. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize Union-Find with V vertices. 2. For each edge (u, v), union u and v, and increment degrees for both vertices. 3. After processing edges, count vertices with odd degree. 4. Check connectivity: find the root of any vertex with degree > 0, then ensure all vertices with degree > 0 share the same root (or at most one such component exists). 5. Based on odd count and connectivity, return result. Time complexity is O(V+E) due to Union-Find operations (near O(α(V)) per operation) and degree counting. Space is O(V) for parent arrays and degree counts. Common mistakes include forgetting to check connectivity for vertices with degree > 0 only (isolated vertices with degree 0 don't affect Eulerian properties), mishandling graphs with multiple edges or self-loops (each edge contributes to degree count normally), and incorrectly counting odd degrees for Euler path (must be exactly 0 or 2). This feeds into the full problem by integrating all sub-routines into a cohesive algorithm.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize Union-Find for 3 vertices: parent=[0,1,2], degree=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: union(0,1) sets parent[1]=0, degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(0,2) sets parent[2]=0, degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process edge [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0 (already connected), degree[2]=2, degree[0]=2. Final degrees: [2,2,2], all even. Step 5: Check connectivity: vertices with degree>0 are 0,1,2, all have root 0, so connected. Step 6: Odd count=0, connected=true → Euler circuit.

Step 1: Initialize for 4 vertices: parent=[0,1,2,3], degree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: union(0,1) sets parent[1]=0, degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(0,2) sets parent[2]=0, degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, union(0,3) sets parent[3]=0, degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1. Final degrees: [1,2,2,1]. Step 5: Check connectivity: vertices with degree>0 are 0,1,2,3, all have root 0, so connected. Step 6: Odd count=2 (vertices 0 and 3), connected=true → Euler path.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find for Graph Connectivity
ladders.desc: Implement Union-Find to check if all vertices with edges are connected in a graph.
ladders.explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is a data structure that efficiently manages partitions of elements into disjoint sets, supporting union (merge sets) and find (determine set representative) operations. For graph connectivity, we treat vertices as elements and edges as unions between them. After processing all edges, we check if all vertices that have at least one edge (degree > 0) belong to the same set, indicating the graph is connected (or has at most one non-trivial component). The intuition is that connectivity is transitive: if vertex A connects to B and B to C, then A connects to C. Union-Find captures this by maintaining parent pointers and using path compression and union by rank for optimization. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array where each vertex is its own parent. 2. For each edge (u, v), call union(u, v) which finds roots of u and v and links them if different. 3. After all unions, pick any vertex with degree > 0, find its root, and verify all other vertices with degree > 0 have the same root. Time complexity is O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (near constant), plus O(V) for the final check. Space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. Common mistakes include not using path compression (leading to slower finds), forgetting to check only vertices with degree > 0 (isolated vertices don't affect connectivity for Eulerian properties), and mishandling initialization for large vertex counts. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by providing the connectivity check needed to determine Eulerian properties.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, link 1 under 0 (rank 0), parent=[0,0,2,3]. Step 3: Union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, link 2 under 0 (rank 0), parent=[0,0,0,3]. Step 4: Union(2,3): find(2)=0, find(3)=3, link 3 under 0 (rank 0), parent=[0,0,0,0]. Step 5: Check connectivity: vertices with edges are 0,1,2,3, all have root 0 → connected.

Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, link 1 under 0, parent=[0,0,2,3,4]. Step 3: Union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, link 3 under 2, parent=[0,0,2,2,4]. Step 4: Check connectivity: vertices with edges are 0,1,2,3. Root of 0 is 0, root of 2 is 2, different → not connected.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Degree Counting for Eulerian Properties
ladders.desc: Count vertex degrees to determine if a connected graph has Euler circuit or path based on odd degree counts.
ladders.explanation: The core logic for Eulerian graphs relies on degree counting: in an undirected graph, the sum of degrees is twice the number of edges (handshake lemma). For Euler circuits, every vertex must have even degree because you enter and exit each vertex equally along the cycle. For Euler paths, exactly two vertices can have odd degree (the start and end of the path), while all others are even; zero odd degrees also indicates an Euler circuit. The intuition is that odd degrees create endpoints that cannot be balanced in a single cycle. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize degree array of size V to zeros. 2. For each edge (u, v), increment degree[u] and degree[v] (handling multiple edges or self-loops by counting each occurrence). 3. Count how many vertices have odd degree. 4. If odd count is 0, potential Euler circuit; if odd count is 2, potential Euler path; otherwise, neither. This assumes the graph is connected (checked separately). Time complexity is O(E) to process edges and O(V) to count odds, so O(V+E). Space is O(V) for degree array. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle self-loops (which add 2 to the degree of that vertex), miscounting for graphs with multiple edges (each edge contributes independently), and applying this logic without connectivity check (degree condition alone is insufficient). This feeds into L1 and L0 by providing the degree analysis that, combined with connectivity, determines the final Eulerian property.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize degree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1. Step 5: Process [3,0]: degree[3]=2, degree[0]=2. Final degrees: [2,2,2,2]. Step 6: Count odds: all even → odd count=0.

Step 1: Initialize degree=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Final degrees: [1,2,1]. Step 4: Count odds: vertices 0 and 2 have odd degree → odd count=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Union-Find with Path Compression and Union by Rank
ladders.desc: Implement optimized Union-Find operations for efficient set management.
ladders.explanation: Union-Find is a data structure that maintains a collection of disjoint sets, supporting find (determine representative of an element's set) and union (merge two sets). Optimizations like path compression and union by rank ensure near-constant time per operation. Path compression flattens the tree during find operations by making each node point directly to the root, reducing future find times. Union by rank attaches the shorter tree under the taller one to keep the tree balanced, minimizing height. The intuition is to maintain efficient access to set representatives while merging sets without creating deep trees. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array where parent[i] = i, and rank array with zeros. 2. Find(x): if parent[x] != x, recursively set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) and return parent[x]. 3. Union(x, y): find roots rx and ry; if rx != ry, compare ranks: if rank[rx] < rank[ry], set parent[rx] = ry; else if rank[rx] > rank[ry], set parent[ry] = rx; else set parent[ry] = rx and increment rank[rx]. Time complexity is O(α(n)) per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (extremely slow-growing, effectively constant). Space is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly (e.g., using iterative find without compression), forgetting to update ranks during union, and initializing ranks incorrectly. This building block feeds into L2 and L1 by providing the efficient connectivity checking mechanism needed for Eulerian graph analysis.

examples.input: Initialize V=4, then union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1, parent=[0,0,2,3]. Step 3: Union(1,2): find(1)=0 (path compression sets parent[1]=0), find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0, set parent[2]=0, parent=[0,0,0,3]. Step 4: Union(2,3): find(2)=0, find(3)=3, rank[0]=1, rank[3]=0, set parent[3]=0, parent=[0,0,0,0].

examples.input: Initialize V=5, then union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1, parent=[0,0,2,3,4]. Step 3: Union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1, parent=[0,0,2,2,4]. Step 4: Union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, set parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2, parent=[0,0,0,2,4] (note: after path compression in find(2), parent[3] may update if called).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Incrementing Vertex Degrees from Edge List
ladders.desc: Process a list of edges to compute the degree of each vertex in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: In graph theory, the degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it. For an undirected graph represented as an edge list, we compute degrees by iterating through each edge and incrementing the degree count for both endpoints. This basic operation is fundamental for many graph algorithms, including Eulerian checks. The intuition is simple: each edge contributes to the connectivity of two vertices, so we add 1 to each vertex's degree. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an array degree of size V (number of vertices) with all zeros. 2. For each edge (u, v) in the edge list: increment degree[u] by 1 and degree[v] by 1. 3. Handle special cases: if u == v (self-loop), increment degree[u] by 2 because a self-loop contributes two incidences to the same vertex. 4. The final degree array represents the degree of each vertex. Time complexity is O(E) since we process each edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the degree array. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle self-loops correctly (should add 2, not 1), using 1-based indexing when vertices are 0-based, and not initializing the degree array properly. This operation feeds into L3 and L2 by providing the degree counts needed for odd degree analysis in Eulerian graph determination.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize degree=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: increment degree[0] to 1, degree[1] to 1. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: increment degree[1] to 2, degree[2] to 1. Final degree array: [1,2,1].

Step 1: Initialize degree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1. Step 5: Process [3,0]: degree[3]=2, degree[0]=2. Final degree: [2,2,2,2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Edge List
ladders.desc: Understand how an undirected graph is represented using a list of edges.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). For computational problems, we need a way to represent this structure. One simple representation is the edge list, where we store the graph as a list of pairs (u, v), each indicating an edge between vertex u and vertex v. In an undirected graph, the edge (u, v) is the same as (v, u), so order doesn't matter. This representation is intuitive and easy to input, but it can be inefficient for certain operations like checking if an edge exists (which requires scanning the list). The intuition is that each edge directly captures a relationship between two vertices. Step-by-step: 1. Identify the number of vertices V (typically labeled 0 to V-1). 2. Create a list where each element is a pair [u, v] representing an edge. 3. For example, a triangle graph with vertices 0,1,2 might have edge list [[0,1], [1,2], [2,0]]. 4. Self-loops are edges where u == v, like [0,0]. 5. Multiple edges between the same vertices can appear as duplicate pairs. Time and space analysis depends on usage; storing the edge list takes O(E) space. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing when problems expect 0-based, forgetting that edges are undirected (so (u,v) and (v,u) are the same), and misinterpreting input formats. This foundation feeds into L4 and higher levels by providing the basic input structure for degree computation and other graph algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: There are 3 vertices: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects vertex 0 and vertex 1. Step 3: Edge [1,2] connects vertex 1 and vertex 2. Step 4: Edge [2,0] connects vertex 2 and vertex 0. Step 5: This forms a triangle where each vertex is connected to the other two.

Step 1: There are 4 vertices: 0, 1, 2, 3. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge [1,2] connects 1 and 2. Step 4: Edge [2,3] connects 2 and 3. Step 5: This forms a path from vertex 0 to vertex 3 via vertices 1 and 2.

testCases.expected: Graph with two vertices connected by one edge
Graph with vertices 0-1-2 in a line
Graph forming a cycle of 4 vertices
Graph with vertex 0 connected to 1 and 2
Graph with two vertices connected by two parallel edges
Graph with a self-loop at vertex 0
Graph with multiple edges in a triangle

approaches.name: Hierholzer's Algorithm O(E)
approaches.summary: Directly construct an Euler circuit or path if it exists by iteratively finding cycles in the graph. This approach verifies Eulerian properties by building the path rather than just checking conditions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Eulerian Graph Using Hierholzer's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if a graph has an Euler circuit or path and construct it if it exists.
ladders.explanation: Hierholzer's Algorithm solves the Eulerian graph problem by directly constructing an Euler circuit or path if it exists, rather than just checking conditions. The intuition is that in an Eulerian graph, we can iteratively find cycles and stitch them together. First, we check Eulerian conditions: For an undirected graph, all vertices must have even degree for a circuit, or exactly two vertices have odd degree for a path. For a directed graph, all vertices must have equal in-degree and out-degree for a circuit, or one vertex has in-degree = out-degree + 1, another has out-degree = in-degree + 1, and all others are balanced for a path. If conditions are met, we start from an appropriate vertex (any vertex for circuit, odd-degree vertex for path). We perform a DFS-like traversal, marking edges as used, and backtrack to build the circuit. When we get stuck, we insert the partial circuit into the main circuit at the appropriate position. This works because Eulerian graphs are composed of edge-disjoint cycles that can be merged. Time complexity is O(E) since each edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of finding cycles recursively. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs properly (graph must be connected in the undirected case, or have a single strongly connected component in directed case for edges), forgetting to mark edges as used, and incorrect starting vertex selection. Real code pattern: Use adjacency lists with edge removal or visited flags, stack for DFS, and list for the final circuit.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=2, deg(2)=2, all even → circuit possible. Graph is connected. Step 2: Start at vertex 0. Step 3: From 0, go to 1 (remove edge 0-1). Circuit so far: [0,1]. Step 4: From 1, go to 2 (remove edge 1-2). Circuit: [0,1,2]. Step 5: From 2, go to 0 (remove edge 2-0). Circuit: [0,1,2,0]. Step 6: All edges used, final circuit: [0,1,2,0].

Step 1: Check degrees: deg(0)=3, deg(1)=2, deg(2)=3, deg(3)=2. Odd degrees at 0 and 2 → path possible. Graph is connected. Step 2: Start at odd-degree vertex 0. Step 3: From 0, go to 1 (remove 0-1). Path: [0,1]. Step 4: From 1, go to 2 (remove 1-2). Path: [0,1,2]. Step 5: From 2, go to 3 (remove 2-3). Path: [0,1,2,3]. Step 6: From 3, go to 0 (remove 3-0). Path: [0,1,2,3,0]. Step 7: From 0, go to 2 (remove 0-2). Path: [0,1,2,3,0,2]. Step 8: From 2, stuck? Check: edge 2-0 already used, no other edges from 2. Backtrack: Insert partial circuit at appropriate point. Final path: [1,0,2,1,3,2,0,3] (starting from 1, another odd vertex).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Cycle Finding in Hierholzer's Algorithm
ladders.desc: The DFS-based function to find a cycle starting from a vertex and return it as a list.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of Hierholzer's Algorithm, responsible for finding a cycle in the graph from a given starting vertex. The intuition is that in an Eulerian graph, from any vertex with unused edges, we can traverse edges until we return to the starting vertex, forming a cycle. We use a DFS approach: start at a vertex, repeatedly move to an adjacent vertex via an unused edge, marking edges as used, and stop when we return to the start. This works because Eulerian graphs have properties ensuring such cycles exist when edges are available. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty list for the cycle. 2. Set current vertex to start. 3. While there is an unused edge from current vertex: a. Pick an unused edge (e.g., the first available). b. Mark it as used. c. Move to the other endpoint. d. Add the vertex to the cycle. 4. Return the cycle. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of edges in the cycle, and overall O(E) across all calls. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to build the Euler circuit. Common mistakes include not marking edges bidirectionally in undirected graphs, infinite loops if edges aren't marked, and incorrect cycle detection. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list with a stack or recursion, and a visited edges set.

examples.input: Graph: vertices=4, edges=[[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,0]], start=0, unused edges all
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, cycle=[0]. Step 2: From 0, unused edge to 1, mark used, move to 1, cycle=[0,1]. Step 3: From 1, unused edge to 2, mark used, move to 2, cycle=[0,1,2]. Step 4: From 2, unused edge to 3, mark used, move to 3, cycle=[0,1,2,3]. Step 5: From 3, unused edge to 0, mark used, move to 0, cycle=[0,1,2,3,0]. Step 6: Back at start, return cycle.

examples.input: Graph: vertices=3, edges=[[0,1],[1,2],[2,0],[0,2]], start=0, unused edges all
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, cycle=[0]. Step 2: From 0, pick edge to 1 (first unused), mark used, move to 1, cycle=[0,1]. Step 3: From 1, edge to 2, mark used, move to 2, cycle=[0,1,2]. Step 4: From 2, edge to 0, mark used, move to 0, cycle=[0,1,2,0]. Step 5: Back at start, return cycle. Note: Could also pick edge 0-2 first for a different cycle.

testCases.expected: Cycle: [0,1] (if considered as cycle in path context)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Stitching Cycles to Form Euler Circuit
ladders.desc: The technique to merge multiple cycles into a single Euler circuit using a stack or recursion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves combining cycles found in the graph to construct an Euler circuit. The intuition is that an Eulerian graph can be decomposed into edge-disjoint cycles, and Hierholzer's Algorithm builds the circuit by recursively finding cycles and inserting them into a main circuit. We use a stack-based approach: start with an empty circuit and a stack containing the starting vertex. While the stack is not empty, pop a vertex; if it has unused edges, find a cycle starting from it using the L1 subroutine, and push the vertices of this cycle onto the stack in reverse order (except the start, which is already on stack). This effectively stitches cycles together. When a vertex has no unused edges, add it to the circuit. This works because each cycle found is a closed loop that can be inserted at the point where we started that cycle in the main traversal. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize stack with start vertex, circuit as empty list. 2. While stack not empty: a. Let current = top of stack. b. If current has unused edges: i. Find a cycle from current (using L1). ii. Push vertices of cycle onto stack in reverse (excluding the start). c. Else: pop from stack and append to circuit. 3. Reverse circuit to get correct order. Time O(E), space O(V+E). Connection to L1: This logic calls the cycle-finding subroutine. Common mistakes: Incorrect stack management leading to wrong order, not reversing circuit, or not handling the start vertex properly. Real code pattern: Use a list as stack and a list for circuit, with while loop.

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[0], circuit=[]. Step 2: Current=0 has unused edges. Find cycle from 0: e.g., [0,1,2,0]. Push vertices in reverse: stack becomes [0,2,1,0]. Step 3: Pop 0, has unused edges? Yes (edge 0-2 and 0-3). Find cycle from 0: e.g., [0,2,3,0]. Push reverse: stack becomes [0,2,1,0,3,2,0]. Step 4: Pop 0, no unused edges? Check: all edges from 0 used. Pop and add to circuit: circuit=[0]. Step 5: Pop 2, no unused edges? Add to circuit: circuit=[0,2]. Continue: final circuit after pops: [0,2,3,0,1,2,0], reverse to get [0,1,2,0,3,2,0].

Step 1: Stack=[0], circuit=[]. Step 2: Current=0 has unused edges. Find cycle from 0: [0,1,2,0]. Push reverse: stack=[0,2,1,0]. Step 3: Pop 0, no unused edges? All used, add to circuit: circuit=[0]. Step 4: Pop 1, no unused edges, add: circuit=[0,1]. Step 5: Pop 2, add: circuit=[0,1,2]. Step 6: Pop 0, add: circuit=[0,1,2,0]. Reverse not needed as order is correct.

testCases.expected: Circuit: [0,1,0] (disconnected, but logic works on component)

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Eulerian Conditions (Degree Parity)
ladders.desc: Determine if a graph meets the necessary conditions for an Euler circuit or path based on vertex degrees.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves verifying the degree conditions that must be satisfied for an Euler circuit or path to exist. The intuition comes from Euler's theorem: For an undirected graph, an Euler circuit exists if and only if all vertices have even degree and the graph is connected (ignoring isolated vertices). An Euler path exists if exactly two vertices have odd degree (which will be the endpoints) and the graph is connected. For directed graphs, replace degree with in-degree and out-degree: circuit requires in-degree equals out-degree for all vertices, and the graph is strongly connected on edges; path requires one vertex with in-degree = out-degree + 1 (end), one with out-degree = in-degree + 1 (start), and others balanced. We check these by computing degrees from the edge list. Step-by-step: 1. Compute degree for each vertex (for undirected, increment for both endpoints; for directed, increment out-degree for source, in-degree for target). 2. Count odd-degree vertices (for undirected) or check in/out differences (for directed). 3. Verify connectivity (for undirected, use DFS/BFS on non-isolated vertices; for directed, check strong connectivity on edges). Time O(V+E), space O(V). Connection to L2: This check is performed before applying cycle-stitching logic to ensure the graph is Eulerian. Common mistakes: Forgetting connectivity check, miscounting degrees for self-loops, or not handling directed graphs correctly. Real code pattern: Use arrays for degrees, and a DFS for connectivity.

examples.output: All degrees even: [2,2,2,2], connected → circuit possible
examples.trace: Step 1: Compute degrees: deg(0)=2 (edges to 1 and 3), deg(1)=2 (0 and 2), deg(2)=2 (1 and 3), deg(3)=2 (2 and 0). Step 2: All even. Step 3: Check connectivity: DFS from 0 visits all vertices → connected. Result: Euler circuit possible.

examples.output: Odd degrees: deg(0)=1, deg(1)=2, deg(2)=2, deg(3)=1, exactly two odd → path possible
examples.trace: Step 1: Compute degrees: deg(0)=1 (edge to 1), deg(1)=2 (edges to 0 and 2), deg(2)=2 (edges to 1 and 3), deg(3)=1 (edge to 2). Step 2: Odd vertices: 0 and 3, count=2. Step 3: Check connectivity: DFS from 0 visits all → connected. Result: Euler path possible.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing and Marking Edges in a Graph
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of moving between vertices via edges and tracking used edges.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves navigating a graph by following edges and marking them as used to prevent re-traversal. The intuition is that in graph algorithms, we often need to explore edges exactly once, and marking ensures we don't get stuck in loops or miss edges. For an undirected graph, an edge between u and v is represented as two directed edges (u,v) and (v,u), and marking one should mark both. We can use adjacency lists with removal of edges or a visited set. Step-by-step: 1. Represent graph as adjacency list (list of neighbors for each vertex). 2. To traverse from vertex u: a. Check if there are any neighbors in adjacency list for u. b. Pick a neighbor v (e.g., the last one for O(1) removal). c. Remove v from u's list and u from v's list (for undirected). d. Move to v. Time O(1) per edge traversal, space O(E) for adjacency list. Connection to L3: This operation is used when checking degrees and during cycle finding. Common mistakes: Not updating both endpoints in undirected graphs, using inefficient data structures leading to O(E) per edge removal, or not handling empty lists. Real code pattern: Use a dictionary or list of lists for adjacency, with pop() for removal.

examples.input: Graph: adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[0], 2:[0]}, start at vertex 0, traverse one edge
examples.trace: Step 1: At vertex 0, neighbors are [1,2]. Step 2: Pick neighbor 1 (e.g., the last). Step 3: Remove 1 from 0's list: 0's list becomes [2]. Step 4: Remove 0 from 1's list: 1's list becomes []. Step 5: Move to vertex 1.

examples.input: Graph: adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}, start at 1, traverse edge to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: At vertex 1, neighbors are [0,2]. Step 2: Pick neighbor 2. Step 3: Remove 2 from 1's list: 1's list becomes [0]. Step 4: Remove 1 from 2's list: 2's list becomes []. Step 5: Move to vertex 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understanding graphs as collections of vertices and edges, and key terms like degree, circuit, and path.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic concepts of graph theory needed for Eulerian problems. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). Graphs can be undirected (edges have no direction) or directed (edges have direction). Key terminology: Degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it (for undirected, count each edge once per endpoint; for directed, in-degree is edges coming in, out-degree going out). An Euler circuit (or cycle) is a path that visits every edge exactly once and starts and ends at the same vertex. An Euler path (or trail) visits every edge exactly once but may start and end at different vertices. The intuition is that these paths use all edges without repetition. We represent graphs using adjacency lists (list of neighbors for each vertex) or adjacency matrices. For example, an undirected graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges (0,1) and (1,2) has adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]. Time to access neighbors is O(degree), space O(V+E). Connection to L4: This representation is used for traversing and marking edges. Common mistakes: Confusing vertices and edges, miscounting degrees, or not understanding directed vs. undirected. Real code pattern: Use a list of lists in Python, e.g., graph = [[] for _ in range(V)].

examples.output: Undirected graph: adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]; degrees: deg(0)=1, deg(1)=2, deg(2)=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices are 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects 0 and 1: add 1 to 0's list, add 0 to 1's list. Step 3: Edge [1,2] connects 1 and 2: add 2 to 1's list, add 1 to 2's list. Step 4: Final adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]. Step 5: Compute degrees: deg(0)=1 (edge to 1), deg(1)=2 (edges to 0 and 2), deg(2)=1 (edge to 1).

Step 1: Vertices 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: 0 adds 1, 1 adds 0. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: 1 adds 2, 2 adds 1. Step 4: Edge [2,3]: 2 adds 3, 3 adds 2. Step 5: Edge [3,0]: 3 adds 0, 0 adds 3. Step 6: Adjacency: 0:[1,3], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]. Step 7: Degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=2, deg(2)=2, deg(3)=2.
